title: ‘Brave assumptions’: FWC queries inflation forecasts
full_text:
Fair Work Commission president Adam Hatcher says Reserve Bank and Treasury forecasts of lower inflation next year were based on “brave assumptions”, as the government left it open for the tribunal to award a pay rise below 5 per cent despite supporting a “real” wage increase for three million workers.
At a final hearing ahead of the commission determining this year’s increase, ACTU senior legal and industrial officer Alister Kentish pressed the union movement’s upgraded 6 per cent wage claim, highlighting how inflation was forecast to hit 5 per cent by June 30 before moderating over the next year , according to the federal budget and the RBA.
Referring to the unresolved Middle East conflict, Justice Hatcher asked Mr Kentish: “That inflation prediction is based on some brave assumptions about external events, isn’t it?
Mr Kentish: “That inflation prediction is based on the best available material to the RBA and the commonwealth. We do, of course, accept there is a degree of uncertainty going forward Justice Hatcher: “Best available data. What’s that? Truth Social, is it?” Mr Kentish: “With respect, your honour, hopefully, the commonwealth can do better than Truth Social.” After the budget lifted forecast inflation for 2025-26 to 5 per cent, the ACTU increased its claim to 6 per cent and the government reiterated its support for an “economically sustainable real” wage increase, which was interpreted as an increase at least above the latest inflation rate of 4.6 per cent.
The budget also forecast inflation would fall to 2.5 per cent over the next financial year.
Under questioning from the FWC expert panel, Matt Cowgill, chief economist for the Department of Employment and Workplace Relations, said: “Not only is the government submission and position not specific about the time period over which inflation should be assessed when calculating a real wage increase, it is also not specific about the inflation measure the (FWC) should use.” He said there were several different measures, including headline inflation, which was 4.6 per cent for the 12 months to March, and trimmed means CPI, which was 3.3 per cent. “The government doesn’t take a position as to which of those measures, over which period, is the specific measure (to) be used to assess whether an economically sustainable real wage increase (has been granted).” Referring to the March figure, Justice Hatcher earlier asked whether the government was saying a real increase would be an amount “north of 4.6 per cent”.
Mr Cowgill maintained it was the practice of the commonwealth not to advocate for a specific percentage increase, and the quantum to be awarded was a matter for the expert panel to determine.
The commission asked him whether an increase below 5 per cent would not meet the government’s objective of a real wage increase. “I’m afraid I’m going to give the same answer which is, the government, consistent with past practice, does not have a position as to the specific quantum that the expert panel should award.” Pressed further that an amount below 4.8-5 per cent would constitute a real wage cut, Mr Cowgill replied “That’s arguable” before proposing his colleague, assistant Treasury secretary, Chris Perks, elaborate. After describing through the year calculations as “jiggery pokery”, the commission pushed Mr Perks on whether 5 per cent was the threshold.
He said it would depend on whether the pay rise was for the year past or year coming when inflation was forecast to fall to 2.5 per cent.
Justice Hatcher: “What do you say we should do? Look forward or look back? Mr Perks: “To be honest, I don’t think there is one definitive approach to this.”
summary: The Fair Work Commission is questioning the government's inflation forecasts as it considers a wage rise for workers. The ACTU is pushing for a 6% increase, while the government suggests a rise below 5% could still be acceptable.
author: EWIN HANNAN
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: FWC President Adam Hatcher criticizes inflation forecasts as overly optimistic.
ACTU demands a 6% wage increase due to expected inflation of 5%.
Government supports a 'real' wage increase but does not specify a percentage.
Different inflation measures complicate the wage increase decision.
Experts suggest the timing of inflation assessment affects wage calculations.
ticker_line: FWC questions government inflation forecasts for wage rise
